---
title: "Day 3 — Exercise: A genomic interval struct"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Define your first Rust [`struct`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch05-01-defining-structs.html) and [`enum`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-01-defining-an-enum.html):

```rust
enum Strand { Plus, Minus }

struct GenomicInterval {
    chrom: String,
    start: u64,
    end: u64,
    strand: Strand,
}
```

Then implement two methods on the struct:

- `length(&self) -> u64` — the size of the interval in base pairs.
- `overlaps(&self, other: &GenomicInterval) -> bool` — whether two intervals overlap, treating coordinates as half-open and respecting chromosome.

## Background

A genomic interval is the most basic spatial data type in bioinformatics. Every BED record, every gene annotation, every variant call ultimately resolves to "chromosome X, position a to position b, on this strand". Coordinates are **half-open**: an interval `[100, 200)` covers 100 base pairs and starts at position 100, but does *not* include position 200. The interval `[200, 300)` starts where the previous one ends and they do **not** overlap.

We will build BED parsing on top of this struct on day 4.

## The exercise

```bash
cd day3/ex-genomic-interval
```

Define `Strand`, `GenomicInterval`, and the two methods. Make all six tests pass.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
cargo run -- chr1 1000 2000 +    # prints length and a self-overlap check
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — defining the enum"}
A C-style [`enum`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-01-defining-an-enum.html) lists its variants with no associated data. Derive [`Debug`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fmt/trait.Debug.html), [`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/clone/trait.Clone.html), [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/marker/trait.Copy.html), and [`PartialEq`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/cmp/trait.PartialEq.html) so it can be printed, copied, and compared:

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum Strand {
    Plus,
    Minus,
}
```

`Copy` is appropriate here because `Strand` is a single byte tag — copying it is free. Larger types should usually be `Clone` only. (See [the Rust Book on `Copy` vs `Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch04-01-what-is-ownership.html#stack-only-data-copy).)
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — defining the struct"}
A [`struct`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch05-01-defining-structs.html) bundles named fields. Derive `Debug`, `Clone`, and `PartialEq` (but **not** `Copy` — `String` is not `Copy`):

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
struct GenomicInterval {
    chrom: String,
    start: u64,
    end: u64,
    strand: Strand,
}
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — implementing methods"}
Methods live inside an [`impl`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch05-03-method-syntax.html) block. The first argument is one of `self`, `&self`, or `&mut self` depending on whether the method consumes, reads, or modifies the value:

```rust
impl GenomicInterval {
    fn length(&self) -> u64 {
        self.end - self.start
    }

    fn overlaps(&self, other: &GenomicInterval) -> bool {
        // TODO
    }
}
```

The half-open overlap rule: two intervals `[a, b)` and `[c, d)` overlap iff `a < d && c < b`. (Adjacency — where `b == c` — is *not* overlap.) And of course they must be on the same chromosome.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
enum Strand {
    Plus,
    Minus,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
struct GenomicInterval {
    chrom: String,
    start: u64,
    end: u64,
    strand: Strand,
}

impl GenomicInterval {
    fn length(&self) -> u64 {
        self.end - self.start
    }

    fn overlaps(&self, other: &GenomicInterval) -> bool {
        self.chrom == other.chrom
            && self.start < other.end
            && other.start < self.end
    }
}
```

Full reference in `day3/ex-genomic-interval-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Next exercise (02)**: parse strings like `"chr1:1000-2000"` into the bare components of an interval, with a custom error type for malformed input.
- **Day 4**: read a real BED file (one interval per line) into a `Vec<GenomicInterval>`, find overlaps, and write the result back out.
